summary: Joystick + crisp HiDPI render + real 2D character sprites for the Phaser survivor game

joystick.approach: Hand-rolled floating VirtualJoystick class (Phaser Graphics, no plugin): base ring spawns at first pointer-down, thumb follows the drag clamped to a max radius, exposes a .vector getter ({x,y} normalized to [-1,1] with analog magnitude up to 1), auto-hides on release. Locks to the originating pointer.id so it ignores other touches. Works for touch + mouse via the same Scene pointer events. moverPlayer() reads joystick.vector OR'd with WASD (WASD wins / sums), replacing the old hold-toward-pointer logic.

crisp.explanation: The blur/mosaic is not an SVG problem and not fixed by the dead `resolution` config key — it is that FIT mode renders a 540x960 WebGL drawing buffer and then CSS-stretches it across a 2x/3x physical screen, so the browser upscales a low-res buffer. Fix = make the drawing buffer match device pixels: set scale width/height to LOGICAL*devicePixelRatio (DPR capped at 2 to protect fill-rate) and use zoom:1/DPR so the on-screen size and all your existing 540x960 world coordinates stay identical. Keep FIT + CENTER_BOTH for letterboxing, add autoRound:true to avoid fractional-pixel CSS smear, and keep pixelArt:false / antialias:true (this game is smooth circles + SVG, not pixel art — pixelArt:true would make it blocky). For SVGs, load.svg's {width,height} in 3.80.1 directly sets the rasterized texture's pixel size (confirmed in the dist source: it rewrites the SVG width/height attributes before decoding), so rasterize at display*DPR (with ~2x headroom) and setDisplaySize down — downscaling a hi-res texture stays crisp. Tradeoffs: the larger buffer (e.g. 1080x1920 at DPR 2) increases GPU fill-rate cost and texture memory (~4x at 2x), which is why DPR is capped at 2 — going to a phone's full 3x rarely looks better but noticeably hurts FPS on this many sprites; and hi-res SVG rasterization uses more texture memory per asset. Per-Text-object `resolution: DPR` is still needed for crisp HUD text since the global resolution key is non-functional.

artSets.palette: Hero indigo/cyan mage (#5b6ee1 robe, #8be9fd staff glow); enemy_basic toxic green slime (#7ed957/#3a8c2a); enemy_fast violet bat-imp (#9b59ff/#6a2fb5); enemy_tank slate-grey stone golem (#8a93a3/#5a626f); gem cyan XP crystal (#33e6ff/#0aa3c2); projectile arcane energy bolt (#7df9ff/#4a6bff). Bold near-black outlines (#1a1a2e) on every sprite.

artSets.direction: Neon-arcade / synthwave: each sprite is a bold geometric-but-clearly-creature form on transparent bg, built with a dark near-black outline for silhouette readability at ~32px, flat fills layered with a single radial glow halo behind it plus thin bright rim-light strokes on the leading edges. Color-coded by role (hero cyan, basic magenta, fast yellow, tank red, gem green, projectile white) so they read instantly against a dark playfield.
artSets.palette: Dark playfield (#0b0a1f). Hero cyan #25f4ee / #0ff with #07303a outline. enemy_basic magenta #ff2bd6 / #b3128f. enemy_fast yellow #ffe000 / #ff9d00. enemy_tank red #ff3b3b / #8c0d12. gem green #3dff88 / #0fae5a. projectile white #ffffff / #bff7ff. All outlines very dark (#0a0a16) for high contrast.

artSets.palette: Chibi-cute monster-collector: candy-bright saturated fills (violet/blue mage, lime slime, hot-pink imp, slate-gray golem, cyan gem, golden star) with deep matching dark outlines for a bold readable silhouette, white eye-glints and pink cheek blush for friendliness.

options.name: npm-published Kenney pack on jsDelivr/unpkg (e.g. kenney-hexagon-pack)
options.license: CC0-1.0 (original Kenney art is CC0/public domain). VERIFY each pack's package.json license field; the art itself is CC0 regardless of wrapper.
options.notes: VERIFIED LIVE & BEST OPTION. Example: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/kenney-hexagon-pack@1.0.0/assets/0.5x/tile1@0.5x.png -> HTTP 200, image/png, access-control-allow-origin: *, content-length 2356, served DIRECTLY from jsDelivr edge cache (no redirect). Same file on unpkg https://unpkg.com/kenney-hexagon-pack@1.0.0/assets/0.5x/tile1@0.5x.png -> HTTP 200 + ACAO: *. Pack has 1133 PNGs incl @0.5x/@1x/@2x density variants. Version-pinning (@1.0.0) gives immutable, infinitely-cacheable URLs (ideal for instant loads). File listing API: https://data.jsdelivr.com/v1/packages/npm/<pkg>@<ver>?structure=flat . CAVEAT: this is a community-published pack, not Kenney-official; pin the version so it can't change/disappear under you, or republish the pack under your own npm scope for full control. Individual tiles 1-10 KB; trivially small.

options.name: Kenney full mirror on GitHub via jsDelivr /gh/ (iwenzhou/kenney 'complete Asset Pack 1')
options.license: CC0-1.0 Universal (repo carries CC0; matches Kenney's public-domain terms). No attribution required.
options.notes: VERIFIED LIVE but with an important catch. Example (path has spaces/parens, must URL-encode): https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/iwenzhou/kenney@master/Art%20(5190%20files)/Archive%20(500%20assets)/Isometric%20road%20assets%20(retired%2C%2070%20assets)/PNG/crossroad.png . Repo is ~21 MB / 5190+ files. jsDelivr does NOT serve this from its own cache — it returns HTTP 301 -> raw.githubusercontent.com (large-repo behavior). The final raw.githubusercontent.com hop IS CORS-safe (returns access-control-allow-origin: * and image/png, verified — 100x65 RGBA PNG, 1500 bytes) but caches weakly (cache-control: max-age=300) and rides on GitHub raw's best-effort availability, not jsDelivr's SLA/edge cache. Folder/file names contain spaces, parentheses and commas -> brittle, must be percent-encoded. Use only if you need the full catalog; otherwise prefer a small focused npm pack.

options.name: phaserjs/examples (official Phaser sample assets) via jsDelivr /gh/ or labs.phaser.io
options.license: Mixed — Phaser engine is MIT; bundled example sprites are demo assets with varied/unclear licensing. NOT safe to assume CC0. Use only for prototyping, not shipping.
options.notes: VERIFIED LIVE. labs.phaser.io/assets/sprites/metalslug_mummy37x45.png -> HTTP 200, image/png, access-control-allow-origin: *, 11596 bytes (works directly, CORS-safe). The /gh/ path 301-redirects to raw.githubusercontent.com (examples repo is ~2 GB) which then serves the PNG with ACAO: *. Convenient because Phaser tutorials set this.load.setBaseURL('https://labs.phaser.io') and load 'assets/sprites/...'. GOOD for prototyping/placeholder art ONLY — licensing of individual demo sprites is not blanket-CC0, so do not ship these in a product.

scout.recommendation: For a game that must load INSTANTLY and look crisp at high DPI, use EXTERNAL CC0-PNG sprite sheets served from a CDN with version-pinned, immutable URLs — NOT embedded SVG — for the actual game art. Reasons: (1) Phaser is a WebGL/Canvas2D raster engine; it uploads bitmaps to GPU textures. It cannot render SVG vectors natively — an SVG must first be rasterized to a canvas at one fixed size, so you lose SVG's resolution-independence the moment it hits the renderer anyway, and you pay a per-image rasterize cost (slower, jankier) on top. PNG sprite atlases are decoded straight to GPU textures and batch-render extremely fast. (2) 'Crisp/high-res' is best served by shipping @2x/@3x density PNG variants (the kenney-hexagon-pack already includes @0.5x/@1x/@2x) and letting Phaser pick by devicePixelRatio, or by packing many sprites into one texture-atlas PNG + JSON so the whole sheet is ONE cacheable HTTP request. (3) 'Instant load' comes from CDN edge caching + version-pinned immutable URLs (e.g. https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/<pkg>@1.0.0/...), which are cached forever at the edge and in the browser, plus HTTP/2 multiplexing. A verbose inline SVG, by contrast, bloats your HTML/JS bundle (blocking first paint) and isn't separately cacheable. PRACTICAL CHOICE & SAFETY NOTES: prefer a version-PINNED npm-published Kenney pack on jsDelivr (verified direct-serve, HTTP 200, ACAO: *, no redirect) over a GitHub /gh/ full-mirror (verified to 301-redirect to raw.githubusercontent.com, which loses jsDelivr's edge cache and has weak max-age=300 caching). To eliminate third-party-availability risk entirely for a game you ship, republish the CC0 PNGs under your OWN npm package / CDN path so the immutable URL is under your control. RESERVE embedded SVG only for tiny, single-color, infinitely-scalable UI chrome (icons, the cursor, HUD glyphs) where vector crispness at arbitrary size matters and the markup is small — keep all sprite/world/character art as external CC0 PNG atlases."
